The publisher can decide, wether a game is a product or a service AFTER THE SALE. And this is all legal at this point.

paragraphs.text: Dear European Comission, I'd like to raise your attention to a problem that I think might be of interest to you, and I would like to ask for your help. I have purchased a software, a video game, called The Crew. After playing it with my girlfriend for countless hours it kinda became our winding down activity when we couldnt go out. Then in the beginning of 2024, the game became unavailable. It turns out the the publisher simply "pulled the plug" and shut the servers down, leaving us no way to even load it up. It wasnt just restricted, it was practically "taken away". It was rather annoying, because we really loved it, and wasnt exactly cheap, but I didnt give much thought to it, life goes on. Then I realized, that it wasnt a one time thing so I did some digging. Hundreds of games have been rendered unplayable, on many occasions right before the launch of the sequel, presumably to maximize profits. And here is the part where I would kindly ask you to take a look into this, because as for my understanding, this is still a gray area legally speaking. The publisher can decide, wether a game is a product or a service AFTER THE SALE. And this is all legal at this point. They do this in many shapes or forms, but the point is, if they are selling a product, and I am the owner of said product, it shouldnt be taken away. After my research I found that many other game's official support has been shut down, but in many cases, the studio gave the community the tools to either host their own servers, or they removed the online requirements for single player games. This only means, that I wasnt required to be online, to play a 100% offline single player game anymore. I think this should be cleared up and laws should be made, because even if they are "only" video games, they are part of our culture, not to mention the often times 40-60-80 EUR price that we pay for them. Just imagine a world, where every 2 years the carrier would compltely shut down every mobile phone, forcing the users to buy the latest ones. They are talking about security concerns and many other made up reasons to get away with it, but since smaller companies already came up with 100% free solutions, and no houses burned down because of a removed DRM, I think its clear that this is just the abuse of the lack of laws around this market. Its not just about the current situation, but more about where it leads. I dont want my 40EUR back, I want to be able to boot up the game that we played and enjoyed years from now, to relive the memories and have a laugh. And I have a feeling Im not alone with this. The problem is out there, the painless and free solution is out there as well, now we need straightforward laws that will enforce these huge companies to not turn everything into a subscription service, especially when they are not communicating this in ther EULA, because they simply dont have to. I would like to thank you for reading my letter, and also would like to kindly ask you to take a look into it, see for yourself, and see if there is a way to preserve our digital products and our right for ownership over something we bought with our hard earned money. Wishing you the best, Gergely Lázár 3300 Eger, Hungary sv.geriger@gmail.com
